messages.content: Hi there
Hello! How can I assist you today?

Here is the conversation transcript between Joe, John and Varun (that is me). The context of the below conversation is as follows:
Context: Non-functional team (NFT) and Accessibility Centre of Excellence (ACoE) team need to collaborate as ACoE team want to setup the Centre of Excellence within an organisation. NFT team is responsible for providing various testers to the project team including accessibility testers.

Conversation Transcript:
John: Hi Joe, You was going back up the management chain to get a view on the overarching NFT team covering the gap between the level of assurance provided from a project handed to the CoE.
 
Joe: We spoke with Struan about the A11y Testers on the most part being a pool based out of NFT.  I think we have agreement in principle.  We did agree that most agile squads would need an A11y Tester permanently embedded in a squad - this would be required to truly shift left on RLGs A11y compliance as a whole.  I believe it will become clearer with time just how many A11y Testers will be required.  I suspect you guys are probably a bit optimistic on the number and the ACoE is pessimistic on the number (i.e. need too many)  - let's not worry about the specifics of that at the moment.
Varun and I are still actively building out the Roadmap for ACoE and have made good headway.  We will come back to you once we've got that and can share plans - Next week is my guess.
 
John: Embedding a specific Accessibility tester into each squad might not be the right approach. We are just ready to roll out a guide to what the performance team service can offer which can be used for Accessibility. Manage - Build, run and maintain the performance test suite, Support - Help build the test suite, provide guidance to the team/project on how to take things forward, Consult - the team/project are skilled up to build, run and maintain their performance suite. If a squad brings in a skilled accessibility tester to build, run and maintain their test suite. Once complete, they will have an expensive resource, at best, doing a side role when there is not accessibility testing to do. What the NFT team is offering is to engage with the team, provide hands on when needed, support through the process of becoming compliant and providing training/upskilling to whoever wants to learn on an ongoing basis.
 
Joe: I've asked Varun Padiyar if he is able to work this through. Varun, I think it would be helpful if we could have visibility of the guide John is talking about.  Can you please send this to us John White? One note before I let Varun continue ... I dare say that you may be over simplifying by saying "Build, run and maintain" the suite.  Yes, automation sort of runs itself once it is set up, and people are paying attention to the results.  About 50% of Accessibility testing is manual testing and will be ongoing throughout the life of a product.  We need to set ourselves up so that each agile squad has the ability to carry out necessary manual A11y testing for each of their work items in their sprints.  Otherwise they will try to release a product that has not been fully A11y tested.
 
John:
Hi Joe, when I say maintain, I'm talking about the whole process. For each manual test, there should be a script that is followed so the test is repeatable, there should be knowledge transfer and upskilling, and anything that is automated there should a maintenance wrapper around it. The whole test piece in this area should have the relevant attention paid to it as you would expect with anything as critical, carrying such a potential impact on credibility, as Accessibility. I think we might be getting our wires crossed here. Also, we need to make sure that change which falls into a waterfall'ish type of bucket is also covered.
 
Varun: Hi John, thanks for sharing your thoughts. It’s great to have your expertise as we figure out how to best integrate non-functional testing services within our teams and ACoE.
I wanted to touch on why we might really benefit from having someone focus just on accessibility within each team:
1. Accessibility is specialised and having someone dedicated means we’re always on the ball with the latest guidelines.
2. With a dedicated tester, we can make sure accessibility gets the continuous attention it needs, which is crucial.
3. When we’re working quickly in Agile, having an expert around means we can sort any issues out straight away.
4. If someone’s trying to handle both accessibility and performance testing, they might not be able to give each area enough attention. Accessibility needs a full-time eye.
5. A tester who knows accessibility inside out will always keep our users’ needs at the forefront, making sure our products are inclusive.
Now, regarding the Waterfall projects, I fully recognise that their structure is more linear than Agile. However, the benefits of having a dedicated accessibility tester are just as important, if not more so. Waterfall projects can sometimes silo different phases such as design, development, and testing. A dedicated accessibility tester ensures that at each stage, accessibility requirements are clearly defined and met before moving to the next phase. This minimises the risk of discovering major accessibility issues late in the project when they are more costly and time-consuming to fix.
Having someone consistently championing accessibility throughout the Waterfall process embeds it into the project from the very beginning. This helps prevent any last-minute surprises that could delay project timelines and ensures a high-quality product for all users.
Let’s keep working together to create a process that supports both Agile and Waterfall methods, helping RLG lead the way in inclusive design.
 
John: Cheers for the above Varun, much appreciated. We might be tackling this in the wrong way. We shouldn't be thinking of working in a waterfall or agile way, Accessibility needs to be embedded in the work to be done from the start and, as we agreed, that needs the right lens applying. Embedding 'Accessibility' from the earliest point is key.
 
Varun: Yes John, completely agree with your thought. We always recommend that accessibility should be included right from the "ideation" phase.
 
John: The framework looks to put the onerous on project to get to a point in time where they have completed their assurance work, then sending this to the CoE to validate. This has a potential to create a bottle neck.Aside from that, embedding an Accessibility SME tester to each team/squad/project would not only be very costly but inefficient. A change could have no impact on Accessibility. The expensive SME would either be sat doing nothing or providing support for work that is below their pay grade. I'm not saying that an SME shouldn't be budgeted for, I'm saying it needs to be thought about and cost effective, which is something I've done on the performance area of NFT.
 
John: On Tuesday. myself and Varun had a conversation around if there was a need for the NFT team to provide any support in the process that change goes through on it's path to live and ticking all the boxes for Accessibility. Points captured from the meeting on Tuesday: -
1. Agreed that there is a skills gap at the moment with the understanding of Accessibility that teams and projects have, and what needs to be done to be compliant
2. This has the potential to impact project go live
3. Change being delivered in both agile and waterfall methodologies need to be compliant, they both need to be thought about and engage in the process with the ACoE
4. The hand over process and impact needs to be worked through
5. When will a project have to complete their Accessibility artefacts and provide them to the ACoE?
6. If this point is a large amount of time before go live this will impact agile projects
7. If this is small amount of time before go live, if major issues are encountered that require a lot of effort and time to fix, this will impact go live
8. Having a number of Accessibility SMEs in a small team, responsible for NFT, would significantly mitigated the above risks
9. To make this work, who ever is delivering this change should make appropriate adjustments to their budget at the relevant phase
The next step is to work out the engagement process that all parties should follow. This will identify any touch points where there might be friction. Varun, please add to this if you think I have missed anything.
 
Varun: Thanks John. These basically sums up our conversation. I would also like to add some additional points which can be useful:
1. Regarding the responsibility for ensuring accessibility implementation, it is important to clarify that while the ACoE governs accessibility in the organization, the ultimate responsibility lies with the project team. The ACoE is there to provide guidance, support, and ensure compliance, but it is the project team’s responsibility to implement accessibility correctly.
2. If at any point it becomes evident that the project go-live is being impacted by the ACoE process , the project team has the option to raise a deferment request. This request would allow them to proceed with the go-live while ensuring that any accessibility issues are addressed promptly in a future release.

Now I have to create a mail explaining and concluding the overall collaboration between the NFT team and ACoE team. Your task is to analyse the above conversation transcript thoroughly and provide a mail summarising the discussion we have done so far. You should also provide the action items against both ACoE team and NFT team. Make sure that email is brief but ensure that all the critical points are covered. I want this mail to be super clear so that once it is created and sent, there is no confusion or further action to be taken.

Subject: Summary and Next Steps for NFT & ACoE Collaboration

Dear Team,

I hope this email finds you well. Following our recent discussions, I would like to provide a concise summary of our conversation on the collaboration between the Non-Functional Testing (NFT) team and the Accessibility Centre of Excellence (ACoE) team, alongside the action items outlined for both teams.

**Summary of Discussion:**

1. **Alignment on Roles**: There is an agreement in principle that most agile squads will benefit from an embedded Accessibility Tester (A11y Tester) as part of the NFT provisioning to enable a shift left on RLG’s Accessibility compliance.

2. **Roadmap and Capacity Planning**: Varun and Joe are actively building the roadmap for the ACoE. An estimated timeline for a more detailed plan is by next week.

3. **Service Offering Guide**: John has mentioned an imminent rollout of a guide detailing the performance team service that will include accessibility processes. Sharing this guide with the ACoE team is essential to ensure alignment.

4. **Manual Testing Emphasis**: Approximately half of accessibility testing requires manual effort and should not be underestimated in terms of resource planning and skills required.

5. **The Need for Dedicated Accessibility Testers**: Varun highlighted the specialized nature of accessibility and the continuous attention it requires, especially in an Agile framework. This suggests benefits in dedicating resources solely to accessibility.

6. **Methodology Integration**: Both Agile and Waterfall methods need to incorporate accessibility from early stages to avoid bottlenecks and potential delays in project timelines.

7. **Value and Cost Efficiency**: Addressing the balance between the value a dedicated Accessibility SME brings against the cost, and ensuring efficient use of resources across all projects, is critical.

8. **Process and Engagement**: Identification of touchpoints, handover processes, and the project's responsibility vs. the ACoE were discussed as key elements to smooth out.

**Action Items:**

**For the ACoE Team:**
- Finalize and share the detailed ACoE roadmap with stakeholders.
- Review John's service offering guide to integrate NFT services with ACoE objectives.
- Clarify governance roles of ACoE vs. project team responsibilities in implementing accessibility.
- Provide guidance on the deferment request process in case project go-live is impacted.

**For the NFT Team:**
- Share the forthcoming guide on performance and accessibility service offerings with the ACoE team.
- Work on upskilling and training initiatives to bridge the skills gap in accessibility within project teams.
- Engage in developing the process framework to ensure seamless integration of accessibility across both Agile and Waterfall methodologies.

**Conclusion:**
Our joint effort aims to embed accessible practices seamlessly from the ideation phase of projects without creating bottlenecks. The collaboration between our teams is crucial to RLG leading the way in inclusive design.

Please feel free to add any further points I might have missed.

Looking forward to our continued work together.

Best Regards,
<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>

---

Please adjust the name and context to fit the actual environment and hierarchy within your organization, and ensure that any acronyms or internal references are correctly represented when communicating officially.
Hello
Hello again! How can I assist you today? If you have any questions or need further assistance, feel free to let me know.

I need to create an accessibility executive summary for client named RLG organization which will tell the leadership team the tasks we have done so far and will complete in future along with the team details. I will provide you the details in bullet points. Your task is to analyze the details and provide the summary in the proper manner. It should be divided into 3 parts: Executive summary of the tasks, team size involved and roles and responsibilities.
Tasks done so far:
	1. Created accessibility policy
	2. Setting up Accessibility Centre of Excellence (ACoE)
	3. Created Accessibility Framework
	4. Accessibility assessments of some of their digital assets.

Tasks that will be done in future:
	1. Collaborating with project teams to integrate accessibility within their workflow by providing training, guidance and resources.
	2. Providing trainings, webinars, awareness sessions.
	3. Governing the overall accessibility within the RLG organization.
	4. Expanding the ACoE team

Team structure: The RLG's Accessibility Centre of Excellence comprises of two people currently (with the plan of expanding it) which are as follows:
	1. Accessibility Consultant
	2. Accessibility Associate

Roles and responsibilities:
	1. Accessibility Consultant:
		a. Responsible for creating accessibility policy
		b. Responsible for setting up the Accessibility Centre of Excellence
		c. Responsible for creating accessibility framework
		d. Responsible for collaborating with teams to ensure accessibility is integrated into their workflow and digital asset creation
		e. Responsible for governing the overall accessibility with the RLG organization
		f. Responsible for creating training materials and providing trainings
	2. Accessibility Associate:
		a. Responsible for assessment and audit of the RLG's digital assets.
		b. Responsible for guiding the developers and designers
		c. Responsible for creating training materials and providing trainings

Ensure that the summary is not very technical. Make it simple yet brief. Structure the summary properly. Also, please make sure that the summary is not super long.

Subject: RLG Accessibility Initiative - Executive Summary

Dear Leadership Team,

**Executive Summary of Tasks**

The RLG organization has reached significant milestones in enhancing our commitment to accessibility and inclusivity. Notably, we have:

1. **Established Policies and Frameworks**: Established a corporate accessibility policy and created a comprehensive Accessibility Framework to guide our digital initiatives.
   
2. **Inception of the ACoE**: Launched the Accessibility Centre of Excellence (ACoE) to champion accessibility across RLG, reflecting our dedication to setting industry standards.
   
3. **Initial Accessibility Assessments**: Conducted initial accessibility assessments of select digital assets to benchmark our current standing and identify areas for improvement.

Our future endeavors include:

1. **Enhanced Team Engagement**: Foster closer collaboration with project teams to weave accessibility into the fabric of their workflows, supported by strategic training, guidance, and resources.
   
2. **Education and Empowerment**: Roll out an array of training sessions, webinars, and awareness initiatives designed to empower our teams with the knowledge and skills needed to champion accessibility.
   
3. **Continuous Governance**: Ensure sustained governance of accessibility practices within RLG through ongoing oversight, direction, and support from the ACoE.
   
4. **Team Expansion**: Invest in the growth of our ACoE team to meet the growing needs of our organization and continue delivering on our promise of an accessible digital environment for all users.

**Team Size Involved**

Currently, the RLG's Accessibility Centre of Excellence is composed of two dedicated positions:

1. **Accessibility Consultant**: The strategic lead and policy creator, driving the setup of the ACoE.
2. **Accessibility Associate**: The operational specialist focusing on assessments and on-the-ground guidance.

**Roles and Responsibilities**

*Accessibility Consultant*:

- Develops overarching accessibility policy and framework, setting the standard for digital inclusivity.
- Pioneers the establishment and operational excellence of the ACoE.
- Strategizes and implements integration of accessibility practices into project teams’ workflows.
- Oversees governance of accessibility within RLG, assuring compliance and consistency.
- Crafts and conducts educational materials and training programs.

*Accessibility Associate*:

- Performs detailed accessibility assessments and audits to ensure digital assets meet defined standards.
- Provides actionable insights and guidance to developers and designers, fostering skill development.
- Contributes to the creation and delivery of training materials and sessions.

We are positioned to be an exemplar in accessibility standards and look forward to expanding these efforts even further in the future.

Thank you for your attention to this initiative and for your support as RLG continues to lead the path to digital accessibility and inclusion.

Warm regards,

<PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_PERSON>
Accessibility Lead, RLG Organization

---

Note: This summary is crafted to provide an overview to the leadership team without delving into technicalities. Adjust as necessary to better fit the RLG organization and its actual activities.

1.	Enhanced Team Engagement: Foster closer collaboration with project teams to weave accessibility into the fabric of their workflows, supported by strategic training, guidance, and resources.
2.	Education and Empowerment: Roll out an array of training sessions, webinars, and awareness initiatives designed to empower our teams with the knowledge and skills needed to champion accessibility.
3.	Continuous Governance: Ensure sustained governance of accessibility practices within RLG through ongoing oversight, direction, and support from the ACoE.
4.	Team Expansion: Invest in the growth of our ACoE team to meet the growing needs of our organization and continue delivering on our promise of an accessible digital environment for all users.

Simplify the above.

1. **Boosting Team Collaboration**: We're working closely with project teams to make sure they include accessibility from the start. We'll help them learn how by providing training and support.

2. **Learning and Development**: We're planning to offer training sessions, webinars, and events to teach our teams how to make our services accessible to everyone.

3. **Ongoing Oversight**: Our Accessibility Centre of Excellence will keep a steady eye on our progress, guiding and supporting teams to make sure we consistently follow accessibility best practices.

4. **Growing Our Team**: We plan to add more members to our Accessibility Centre of Excellence to keep up with our organization's expanding needs and our commitment to accessible services.

You are an accessibility consultant and expert in governance as well as running accessibility centre of excellence (ACoE). I have written step-by-step guide to achieve accessibility in any project that want to implement accessibility and came to (ACoE) for guidance. Your task is first analyze this step-by-step guide and check whether it is correct or not. Then, based on the step-by-step guide I provided, frame a correct and feasible step-by-step guide which can be used by both ACoE and project teams:
How to get your product accessible
	1. Engage with our Accessibility Centre of Excellence: 
			i. Start your accessibility journey by connecting with the Accessibility Centre of Excellence (ACoE) via email. Think of them as your accessibility experts. Reach out for regular meetings or chats to discuss accessibility topics, ask for advice, and make sure your project is on the right track.
	2. Conduct accessibility assessment: 
			i. An accessibility assessment is like a health check for your product. It helps you identify issues that might make your product hard to use for everyone.
			ii. This can include using assistive technologies like screen readers and manual testing to uncover issues that automated tools such as Deque Axe may miss. Reach out to the ACoE for an accessibility assessment.
			iii. The findings of the assessment should be documented, including a list of identified issues, their severity, and potential solutions.
			iv. Example: The accessibility assessment of an e-commerce website revealed several issues, including missing alternative text for product images, keyboard navigation problems, and colour contrast violations.
	3. Set clear accessibility objectives and roadmap: 
			i. Accessibility objectives should be specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound (SMART). It should outline key milestones, deadlines, and deliverables for achieving accessibility conformance.
			ii. Collaborate with the ACOE to define these objectives, aligning them with the industry standards (e.g. WCAG) ensuring that the objectives address the needs of users with disabilities. Ensure that a clear roadmap is developed and checkpoint for progress evaluation is included.
			iii. Example: An accessibility objective could be, "Achieve WCAG 2.1 Level AA conformance for our website within the next 12 months, addressing all identified accessibility issues".
	4. Training and Awareness: 
			i. Training ensures that all project members understand accessibility principles and how to implement them. Awareness campaigns helps instil the importance of accessibility throughout the organisation.
			ii. Conduct training sessions covering topics like assistive technologies, WCAG guidelines, and accessible design. Reach out to the ACoE for any trainings.
	5. Inclusive Design Integration: 
			i. Inclusive design involves integration of accessibility from the inception of the design phase. It involves considering accessibility in all design decisions to create an inclusive user experience.
			ii. Collaborate closely with designers to ensure that accessibility is an integral part of the design process. Consider factors such as colour choices, font sizes, navigation, and interactive elements.
	6. Accessibility Testing and Validation: 
			i. Testing is a crucial step to ensure that product meets accessibility requirements. It involves automated tools, assistive technologies, and manual evaluations.
			ii. Conduct accessibility testing throughout the development process. Collaborate with ACoE to determine the appropriate testing tools and methodologies for your product. This will be conducted by the accessibility testers on your team. 
	7. Prioritise Accessibility fixes: 
			i. Prioritisation involves categorising accessibility issues based on their impact and severity. High-priority issues that significantly affect users should be addressed first.
			ii. Once the backlog of accessibility issues have been identified, collaborate with ACoE to prioritise the accessibility fixes. Use criteria like potential user impact, legal requirements, and ease of implementation to determine priority.
	8. Documentation and Reporting: 
			i. Documentation is essential for tracking and reporting on accessibility efforts, including testing results, identified issues, and remediation steps.
			ii. Keep comprehensive records of accessibility findings and progress in addressing issues in the form of reports.
	9. Deferral Process: 
			i. In some cases, immediate accessibility conformance may not be achievable. In such instances, you'll need to consider the deferral process we have defined to document deferred issues, their reasons, and plan for future remediation.
			ii. Collaborate with ACoE to understand the deferral process. Please refer to the "Accessibility Deferment" section for more details.
	10. External Audits (if applicable) 
			i. External audits involve engaging third-party experts to conduct independent accessibility assessments of the product.
            ii. Once the product is stable from an accessibility perspective (around 90 – 95% of the accessibility defects have been addressed), schedule an external audit with the recommended vendors to ensure impartial evaluations. Collaborate with ACoE to define the scope, objectives, and timeline for audits.

The step-by-step guide you've provided is comprehensive and generally aligns well with industry standards for developing accessible products. The emphasis on continuous engagement with the Accessibility Centre of Excellence (ACoE) and the mention of both automated and manual testing are noteworthy. The structure also outlines a thoughtful progression from assessment through to external audits, and the addition of a deferral process is important for managing issues that cannot be immediately addressed.

However, in crafting a more structured guide for implementation, we may want to consider the following additions and modifications to ensure it is clear, actionable, and comprehensive for both the ACoE and project teams:

**Revised Step-by-Step Guide for Achieving Accessibility:**

1. **Initial Engagement with ACoE**:
   - Begin by collaborating with the ACoE.
   - Schedule an initial consultation to outline your project’s needs, get expert advice, and establish regular checkpoints.

2. **Accessibility Assessment**:
   - Complete a thorough assessment utilizing both automated tools and manual testing.
   - Document the results, detailing issues, severity, and suggesting solutions for remediation.

3. **Define Accessibility Goals and Roadmap**:
   - With ACoE guidance, establish SMART goals, such as obtaining WCAG 2.1 Level AA compliance within a set timeframe.
   - Create a roadmap with the ACoE, identifying milestones and checkpoints.

4. **Initiate Training and Awareness Programs**:
   - Organize training for team members on accessibility guidelines and implementation strategies.
   - Launch awareness campaigns to embed the value of accessibility across the organization.

5. **Apply Inclusive Design Principles**:
   - Integrate accessibility considerations early in the design phase to ensure inclusivity.
   - Work in tandem with designers to incorporate accessibility into design choices.

6. **Conduct Ongoing Accessibility Testing**:
   - Implement regular accessibility testing, utilizing a mix of automated and manual evaluations.
   - Involve accessibility testers in reviewing your product as it develops.

7. **Prioritize and Address Accessibility Issues**:
   - Classify and tackle accessibility issues by their impact, focusing on high-severity items first.
   - Work with ACoE to strategize on the most effective remediation plan.

8. **Maintain Documentation and Reporting**:
   - Keep track of accessibility efforts, test results, and remediation actions diligently.
   - Regularly report progress to stakeholders and the ACoE.

9. **Understand and Apply the Deferral Process**:
   - Learn about the deferral process for instances where immediate compliance is not viable.
   - Document plans for addressing deferred issues with timelines for completion.

10. **Plan for External Audits**:
    - Once the majority of accessibility issues are resolved, engage a third-party vendor for an independent audit.
    - Work with ACoE to prepare for the audit and act on findings.

Through these carefully designed steps, we can offer a robust framework that guides project teams while also embracing the ACoE's expertise. This synergy is crucial for maintaining high standards of accessibility throughout each phase of a project, and ultimately, delivering an inclusive user experience. The successful implementation of this guide relies on the commitment of all team members and the ACoE's leadership in steering our collective accessibility efforts.

Check step 2 Accessibility assessment and step 6 Conduct Ongoing Accessibility Testing. Do you think they are one and same or not? If they are one and same thing, then provide your suggestion and correct. Then provide the revised Step-by-Step Guide.

Step 2, "Accessibility Assessment," and Step 6, "Conduct Ongoing Accessibility Testing," are related but represent different stages or phases of the accessibility process within a product's lifecycle.

"Accessibility Assessment," as outlined in Step 2, typically refers to an initial review done to understand the current status of a product's accessibility. This is often a comprehensive baseline review that identifies where the product stands in relation to accessibility guidelines and standards, such as the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG).

On the other hand, "Conduct Ongoing Accessibility Testing" in Step 6 involves a continuous process where accessibility testing is integrated into the regular development cycle. This can include iterative testing of new features, regression testing of existing functionalities, and updates based on feedback or changes to accessibility guidelines.

It is important to differentiate between these two steps to ensure a clear understanding that accessibility is not a one-time task but an ongoing commitment throughout development and post-release. However, to ensure clarity and remove redundancy from the guide, we can integrate these two steps into a more cohesive and well-defined continuous testing process.

**Revised Step-by-Step Guide for Achieving Accessibility:**

1. **Initial Engagement with ACoE**:
   - Partner with the ACoE for expert guidance and establish a plan for regular collaboration throughout your project.

2. **Accessibility Evaluation and Roadmap Development**:
   - Perform an initial in-depth accessibility evaluation to identify gaps and areas for improvement.
   - Create an actionable roadmap with clear goals, such as attaining WCAG 2.1 Level AA compliance, including milestones for regular ongoing testing.

3. **Training and Awareness**:
   - Organize comprehensive training for all team members and conduct awareness campaigns to ensure an organization-wide understanding of the importance of accessibility.

4. **Integrate Inclusive Design**:
   - Embed accessibility principles in the design phase, ensuring all design decisions consider accessibility and contribute to an inclusive user experience.

5. **Ongoing Accessibility Testing Throughout the Product Lifecycle**:
   - Implement regular and continuous accessibility testing as part of the development process, leveraging both automated tools and manual evaluations, to maintain and improve accessibility standards consistently.
   - Prioritize and address found issues, starting with those that have the highest impact on users.

6. **Document Progress and Achievements**:
   - Maintain detailed records of accessibility initiatives, testing results, issue remediation, and advancements toward accessibility goals for regular reporting and accountability.

7. **Manage Accessibility Deferrals Responsibly**:
   - Understand the deferral process for areas where immediate compliance is not feasible, ensuring that plans for resolution are detailed and time-bound.

8. **External Accessibility Audit**:
   - Schedule independent third-party audits to validate accessibility compliance and rectify any remaining issues, especially after significant improvements or major releases.

By following this revised guide, teams ensure that accessibility efforts are seen as both foundational and continuous, reflecting an ongoing commitment to inclusivity and adherence to best practices. This refined approach underscores the collaboration between the ACoE and project teams to embed accessibility into the organization's culture and process workflows.
Who is responsible for performing Step 2: Accessibility Evaluation and Roadmap Development and Step 5: Ongoing Accessibility Testing Throughout the Product Lifecycle:

Within the context of the Accessibility Centre of Excellence (ACoE) and a project team collaboration structure, the responsibilities for performing "Accessibility Evaluation and Roadmap Development" (Step 2) and "Ongoing Accessibility Testing Throughout the Product Lifecycle" (Step 5) are as follows:

**Step 2: Accessibility Evaluation and Roadmap Development**
- **Accessibility Consultant** from the ACoE is primarily responsible for:
  - Leading the initial in-depth accessibility evaluation.
  - Identifying gaps and non-conformance with accessibility standards.
  - Developing an accessibility roadmap aligned with organizational goals and compliance standards.

- **Project Team** is responsible for:
  - Providing access to the product and related documentation.
  - Participating in the evaluation process to gain insights and understanding of accessibility requirements.
  - Collaborating with the ACoE in setting realistic goals and timelines for the roadmap.

**Step 5: Ongoing Accessibility Testing Throughout the Product Lifecycle**
- **Accessibility Testers** within the project team are responsible for:
  - Conducting consistent and thorough accessibility testing throughout the development, including automated and manual testing using assistive technologies.
  - Reporting the findings and collaborating with developers and designers to provide accessible solutions.

- **Accessibility Associate** from the ACoE is responsible for:
  - Assisting in the selection of appropriate testing tools and methodologies.
  - Acting as an advisor for best practice approaches during the testing process.
  - Validating or auditing test results at certain milestones for quality assurance.

- **Developers and Designers** on the project team are responsible for:
  - Integrating accessibility considerations into the development and design from the outset.
  - Addressing identified issues based on priority and impact.
  - Ensuring that implemented fixes meet the established accessibility standards.

In this collaborative model, while the ACoE provides expertise, guidance, and validation, the project team ensures that accessibility is incorporated into their day-to-day work. Both teams work in tandem, emphasizing that accessibility is an ongoing process integrated into the product lifecycle, rather than an afterthought or standalone task.
Based on the step-by-step guide I provided and revised step-by-step guide you provide, provide me who is responsible for each step/activity.

Here is a breakdown of the responsibilities for each step within the revised step-by-step guide for achieving accessibility, based on a typical collaboration between an Accessibility Centre of Excellence (ACoE) and project teams:

**1. Initial Engagement with ACoE**:
   - **Project Manager/Team Lead**: Initiates contact, schedules consultation, and ensures ongoing communication with ACoE.
   - **ACoE Lead (Accessibility Consultant/Manager)**: Provides initial consultation, offers expertise, and sets expectations for collaboration.

**2. Accessibility Evaluation and Roadmap Development**:
   - **Accessibility Consultant (ACoE)**: Leads the initial accessibility evaluation, assists in identifying gaps, and helps develop a compliance roadmap.
   - **Project Team**: Participates in the evaluation, supplies necessary information, and commits to following the roadmap.

**3. Training and Awareness**:
   - **ACoE (Consultant and Associate)**: Designs and conducts training sessions and develops awareness campaigns.
   - **Project Team (HR/Training Department)**: Facilitates training attendance and promotes company-wide awareness.

**4. Integrate Inclusive Design**:
   - **Designers and Developers (Project Team)**: Incorporate accessibility from the start, consult ACoE for best practices.
   - **Accessibility Specialist (ACoE/Project Team)**: Provides guidelines and support to ensure inclusive design principles are applied.

**5. Ongoing Accessibility Testing Throughout the Product Lifecycle**:
   - **Accessibility Testers (Project Team)**: Perform testing as part of the development cycle, using a mix of automated and manual evaluations.
   - **ACoE (Consultant and Associate)**: Offers guidance on testing tools/methodologies and validates testing process and results.

**6. Document Progress and Achievements**:
   - **Project Manager**: Ensures detailed documentation and regular progress reporting are maintained.
   - **ACoE**: Reviews documentation for completeness and compliance accuracy.

**7. Manage Accessibility Deferrals Responsibly**:
   - **Project Manager/Team Lead**: Documents deferrals and plans for future remediation.
   - **ACoE Lead (Accessibility Consultant/Manager)**: Advises on the deferral process and oversees resolution plans.

**8. External Accessibility Audit**:
   - **ACoE Lead (Accessibility Consultant/Manager)**: Prepares for and coordinates the external audit.
   - **Project Team**: Implements necessary changes following audit findings, assisted by ACoE input.

In summary, the project team is largely responsible for carrying out the tasks and integrating accessibility into their processes, while the ACoE provides oversight, expert advice, guidance, and validation. This delineation ensures accessibility is a shared responsibility, with clear ownership and support from specialized experts.

Now here is the revised step-by-step guide you provided for achieving accessibility:
	
	1. Initial Engagement with ACoE:
	- Begin by collaborating with the ACoE.
	- Schedule an initial consultation to outline your project’s needs, get expert advice, and establish regular checkpoints.
	
	2. Accessibility Assessment:
	- Complete a thorough assessment utilizing both automated tools and manual testing.
	- Document the results, detailing issues, severity, and suggesting solutions for remediation.
	
	3. Define Accessibility Goals and Roadmap:
	- With ACoE guidance, establish SMART goals, such as obtaining WCAG 2.1 Level AA compliance within a set timeframe.
	- Create a roadmap with the ACoE, identifying milestones and checkpoints.
	
	4. Initiate Training and Awareness Programs:
	- Organize training for team members on accessibility guidelines and implementation strategies.
	- Launch awareness campaigns to embed the value of accessibility across the organization.
	
	5. Apply Inclusive Design Principles:
	- Integrate accessibility considerations early in the design phase to ensure inclusivity.
	- Work in tandem with designers to incorporate accessibility into design choices.
	
	6. Conduct Ongoing Accessibility Testing:
	- Implement regular accessibility testing, utilizing a mix of automated and manual evaluations.
	- Involve accessibility testers in reviewing your product as it develops.
	
	7. Prioritize and Address Accessibility Issues:
	- Classify and tackle accessibility issues by their impact, focusing on high-severity items first.
	- Work with ACoE to strategize on the most effective remediation plan.
	
	8. Maintain Documentation and Reporting:
	- Keep track of accessibility efforts, test results, and remediation actions diligently.
	- Regularly report progress to stakeholders and the ACoE.
	
	9. Understand and Apply the Deferral Process:
	- Learn about the deferral process for instances where immediate compliance is not viable.
	- Document plans for addressing deferred issues with timelines for completion.
	
	10. Plan for External Audits:
	- Once the majority of accessibility issues are resolved, engage a third-party vendor for an independent audit.
	- Work with ACoE to prepare for the audit and act on findings.

Provide roles and responsibilities against each step in terms of ACoE and project teams.

Here is a detailed assignment of roles and responsibilities for the revised step-by-step guide, outlining who is responsible within the Accessibility Centre of Excellence (ACoE) and the project teams for each step in ensuring accessibility:

1. **Initial Engagement with ACoE**:
   - **Project Team**: Reaches out to form a collaborative partnership with the ACoE.
   - **ACoE**: Offers expertise, consults on the project’s accessibility needs, and sets up regular meetings for ongoing advice and check-ins.

2. **Accessibility Assessment**:
   - **Project Team**: Conducts the initial assessment using automated tools, with involvement from developers and quality assurance.
   - **ACoE**: Provides manual testing expertise, assesses complex accessibility concerns, and assists in interpreting results.

3. **Define Accessibility Goals and Roadmap**:
   - **ACoE**: Helps define SMART accessibility goals and contributes to the creation of a strategic roadmap.
   - **Project Team**: Commits to the goals and collaborates on the roadmap development, ensuring all project milestones include accessibility check-ins.

4. **Initiate Training and Awareness Programs**:
   - **ACoE**: Develops training materials and delivers training sessions on accessibility.
   - **Project Team**: Encourages participation in training and integrates the principles learned into everyday work practices across all departments.

5. **Apply Inclusive Design Principles**:
   - **Designers (Project Team)**: Ensures that accessibility is considered in the early design phase and throughout the design process.
   - **ACoE**: Advises on how to apply inclusive design principles effectively and reviews design decisions for accessibility compliance.

6. **Conduct Ongoing Accessibility Testing**:
   - **Accessibility Testers/Quality Assurance (Project Team)**: Conducts regular accessibility testing throughout the development process.
   - **ACoE**: Serves as a resource for testing methodologies, tools, and best practices and validates the efficacy of the test results.

7. **Prioritize and Address Accessibility Issues**:
   - **Project Team**: Prioritizes issues based on impact and works on fixing them, with high-priority issues taking precedence.
   - **ACoE**: Supports the project team in creating an effective remediation strategy and provides expert recommendations for complex issues.

8. **Maintain Documentation and Reporting**:
   - **Project Team (Project Manager/Lead)**: Maintains thorough documentation of accessibility efforts and ensures regular progress reporting.
   - **ACoE**: Reviews reports, ensures that remediation is accurately documented, and provides feedback on progress.

9. **Understand and Apply the Deferral Process**:
   - **Project Team**: Understands and documents any instances where accessibility cannot be immediately addressed, creating a plan for future remediation.
   - **ACoE**: Defines the deferral process and assists in outlining clear plans and timelines for addressing any deferred issues.

10. **Plan for External Audits**:
   - **Project Team**: Coordinates with third-party vendors for an independent audit once ready.
   - **ACoE**: Assists in choosing vendors, defining the scope of the audit, and helps interpret and action the audit findings.

By clearly defining these responsibilities, both the ACoE and project teams can work together effectively towards the goal of creating accessible products. The partnership enables shared accountability, facilitates knowledge transfer, and ensures that both sides are engaged in the process of achieving accessibility compliance.
In terms of accessibility centre of excellence, what are the different requests ACoE can receive?

An Accessibility Centre of Excellence (ACoE) can receive a variety of requests related to the accessibility of digital content, products, and services. These can range from requests for support and guidance to more specific evaluations and remediation efforts. Here are some common types of requests that an ACoE might receive within an organization:

1. **Assessment and Audit Requests**:
   - Requests for accessibility assessments or audits of websites, applications, or other digital assets to identify compliance with standards such as WCAG.

2. **Training and Workshop Requests**:
   - Requests for training sessions, workshops, or webinars on accessibility topics, including understanding accessibility standards, implementing best practices, using assistive technologies, and inclusive design.

3. **Consultation Requests**:
   - Requests for expert advice on specific accessibility challenges, best practices, and strategies for integrating accessibility into existing workflows or during the design phase.

4. **Remediation Guidance Requests**:
   - Requests for assistance in addressing identified accessibility issues, including recommendations for fixes and support during the remediation process.

5. **Tool and Resource Recommendations**:
   - Requests for recommendations on the best tools, plugins, or resources to facilitate the testing, development, and maintenance of accessible content.

6. **Policy and Governance Requests**:
   - Requests for guidance or input on developing or refining organizational accessibility policies, standards, or governance procedures.

7. **Support with Legal Compliance**:
   - Requests for support in understanding and meeting legal requirements and standards for accessibility to minimize the risk of non-compliance and litigation.

8. **Documentation and Reporting Assistance**:
   - Requests for help in creating documentation and reports related to accessibility, such as VPATs (Voluntary Product Accessibility Templates) or accessibility statements for public consumption.

9. **Technology Procurement Consultation**:
   - Requests for consultation during the procurement process to ensure that third-party products and services meet accessibility requirements before purchase or integration.

10. **User Testing Coordination**:
    - Requests for coordinating and conducting user testing with individuals with disabilities to gather feedback on the usability and accessibility of products.

11. **Deferral Process Management**:
    - Requests to understand or apply the process for deferring accessibility fixes, including documentation of issues and planning for future resolution.

12. **Design Review Requests**:
    - Requests for accessibility reviews of prototypes, wireframes, or design assets before they go into development.

13. **Project Collaboration Requests**:
    - Requests for ongoing collaboration with project teams to integrate accessibility into agile methodologies or waterfall project plans.

An ACoE functions as both a strategic and operational resource within an organization, providing a central hub of expertise and support to ensure that products and services are accessible to all users, including people with disabilities. By responding to these diverse requests, an ACoE helps to foster a culture of inclusivity and ensure compliance with accessibility guidelines and laws.

You are an accessibility expert. You have thorough knowledge about all the Material 2 and Material 3 design. I need to create a document detailing relevant and important changes or differences has been created between Material 2 and Material 3 only in terms of accessibility.

As an accessibility expert, it's essential to consider how design systems like Material Design evolve over time, and the impact these changes have on accessibility. Material Design (often referred to as Material 2) has been updated to Material You (commonly known as Material 3), bringing a variety of changes. I will outline some of the key differences and updates with a particular focus on accessibility.

**Please note:** The design concepts might continue to evolve beyond my knowledge cutoff in 2023. Always refer to the latest material design guidelines for the most up-to-date information.

---

**Title: Accessibility Considerations in Material Design: Moving from Material 2 to Material 3**

**Introduction:**
Material Design has progressed from Material 2 to Material 3, significantly improving user experience aesthetics and functionality. This document focuses on the differences between the two versions concerning accessibility aspects.

**Contrast and Color:**
Material 2 emphasized the importance of adequate contrast ratios for readability and provided color palettes that supported a variety of accessibility needs. With Material 3, there's a shift towards more dynamic and personalized color systems, providing greater flexibility for creating interfaces that are both vibrant and accessible.

- **Material 2**: Offered pre-defined color palettes with recommended contrast ratios.
- **Material 3**: Introduces dynamic theming capabilities, allowing more personalized experiences. It requires thorough testing to ensure that generated color schemes do not compromise contrast requirements for text and interface elements.

**Typography and Text Legibility:**
Both versions of Material Design advocate for clear and legible typography, but Material 3 offers a renewed focus on adaptable type scales and responsiveness.

- **Material 2**: Provided guidelines for typographic scale and clarity.
- **Material 3**: Enhances text legibility with more responsive type scales that adapt better to different screen sizes and user preferences. This adaptability can be beneficial for users with visual impairments.

**Touch Targets:**
Ensuring that touch targets are large enough to interact with is essential for users with motor control issues. Material 3 aims to make components more accessible by refining these standards.

- **Material 2**: Had clear guidelines for the size of interactive elements.
- **Material 3**: Continues to emphasize the importance of touch targets that meet minimum size recommendations, and these criteria have been refined to cater to a broader range of devices and contexts.

**Components and Interaction:**
Material 3 makes several changes to components and their interactions, which can affect how users with disabilities engage with interfaces.

- **Material 2**: Included components like buttons, menus, and navigation drawers designed for clear interaction.
- **Material 3**: Updates components with additional states and interactive cues, which should be examined for their accessibility impact. For example, ensure that state changes are perceivable to users with color blindness or low vision.

**Assistive Technologies Compatibility:**
Both versions of Material Design strive for compatibility with assistive technologies, but Material 3 has amplified this commitment with enhanced support.

- **Material 2**: Focused on broad compatibility with screen readers and other assistive technologies.
- **Material 3**: Increases focus on seamless integration with a wider range of assistive technologies, particularly around touch and voice input, providing a more inclusive experience.

**Motion and Animation:**
Material Design has always carefully considered motion, and Material 3 further sophisticates these interactions, emphasizing meaningful and accessible animations.

- **Material 2**: Provided guidelines for motion that added to the user's understanding of the interface.
- **Material 3**: Introduces more nuanced motion patterns. Every motion should be checked to ensure they do not induce motion sickness in users and that they respect the preferences for reduced motion settings.

**Conclusion:**
Material 3 continues the Material Design tradition of thoughtful, inclusive design practices, refining and expanding upon the foundation set by Material 2. By embracing the new dynamic theming, responsive typography, refined touch targets, updated components, compatibility with assistive technologies, and sophisticated motion design, Material 3 is geared towards creating an accessible experience for a diverse range of users.

---

In creating this document, accessibility pros should conduct hands-on testing with Material 3 components, themes, and strategies, adhering to the most current WCAG guidelines and assessing user experience across various disabilities. 

Remember, specific changes should reference the actual Material Design documentation as this information is generalized and may not cover all updates or alterations in detail.
Could you please provide the accessibility testing qualifications that we might look for a good accessibility standard? What will be the gold standard?

To ensure a high standard of accessibility testing, it's vital to hire professionals who possess a combination of formal qualifications, practical experience, and a deep understanding of accessibility guidelines and principles. While specific qualifications can vary depending on the region and the industry, here are some key credentials and skills that are typically regarded as gold standards in the field of accessibility testing:

1. **Certification in Web Accessibility**:
   - International Association of Accessibility Professionals (IAAP) offers certifications like Certified Professional in Accessibility Core Competencies (CPACC) and Web Accessibility Specialist (WAS).
   - These certifications are internationally recognized and demonstrate a broad, foundational knowledge of accessibility concepts and advanced skills in web accessibility solutions.

2. **Knowledge of Accessibility Guidelines and Standards**:
   - Familiarity with the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.1 and understanding its three levels of compliance (A, AA, AAA).
   - Experience with other relevant standards, such as the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA), Section 508, ARIA (Accessible Rich Internet Applications) practices, and EN 301 549.

3. **Technical Expertise**:
   - Proficiency in using accessibility testing tools and assistive technologies, such as screen readers (e.g., JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver), screen magnifiers, speech recognition software, and alternative input devices.
   - Understanding of HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and WAI-ARIA roles and properties to identify and fix accessibility issues in code.

4. **Practical Experience**:
   - Hands-on experience in performing both automated and manual accessibility audits.
   - Experience in creating VPATs (Voluntary Product Accessibility Template) or Accessibility Conformance Reports (ACRs).

5. **Soft Skills**:
   - Empathy and understanding of the different ways users interact with digital content, especially people with disabilities.
   - Strong analytical and problem-solving skills to identify and remediate accessibility issues.

6. **Continuous Learning and Development**:
   - Staying current with new developments, tools, and best practices in the rapidly evolving field of digital accessibility.
   - Engaging with professional accessibility communities, online forums, webinars, and conferences to keep skills updated.

7. **Experience with User-Centered Design and Testing**:
   - Involvement in user testing with people with various disabilities to gather real-world feedback on the usability and accessibility of digital products.
   - Understanding inclusive design principles and integrating them into user experience (UX) design processes.

8. **Legal and Regulatory Awareness**:
   - Knowledge of the legal and regulatory landscape related to digital accessibility.
   - Familiarity with global accessibility laws and how they impact digital products and services in different jurisdictions.

The "gold standard" for an accessibility tester would be a combination of the above qualifications, coupled with a track record of successfully improving the accessibility of web and mobile applications across various platforms. Additionally, a commitment to advocating for users with disabilities and promoting accessibility as an integral aspect of usability and user experience is crucial. An ideal candidate would be one who not only performs accessibility testing but also champions inclusive design and serves as an accessibility advocate within their organization.
